answers.value:
Persona / Workplace Context:
I am completing this form as the Infection Prevention & Employee Health Coordinator for Evergreen Valley Community Hospital, a 150-bed acute-care facility located in Portland, OR. Our hospital includes a 16-bed Emergency Department, two medical-surgical floors, a 10-bed ICU, Labor & Delivery, and several outpatient clinics. We routinely evaluate and treat patients with respiratory symptoms, including suspected and confirmed COVID-19 cases.

Our nursing staff, respiratory therapists, physicians, and some technicians provide direct hands-on care to patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19. This includes tasks such as initial triage in the Emergency Department, ongoing inpatient care on designated COVID-capable units, and critical care support in the ICU. In these settings, staff are within 6 feet of patients for prolonged periods and may have repeated close contact during assessments, medication administration, assistance with activities of daily living, and respiratory support.

AGP-Related Details:
- Open suctioning of airways: This is performed by ICU nurses and respiratory therapists on intubated patients when closed suction systems are not feasible or when patients are not yet intubated but require airway clearance. For example, in our ICU, respiratory therapists may perform open suctioning several times per shift on a COVID-positive patient with copious secretions who is not tolerating a closed suction catheter.

- Sputum induction: We do not routinely perform sputum induction for diagnostic purposes on suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients. Our pulmonologists strongly prefer non-induction methods (e.g., expectorated sputum when possible) in order to minimize aerosol generation. If sputum is required, they usually rely on samples obtained via existing airways (e.g., from an endotracheal tube) rather than inducing sputum in non-intubated patients.

- Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR): CPR is performed as needed on any patient, including those with suspected or confirmed COVID-19. For instance, in 2023–2024 we had several Code Blue events on patients who were either COVID-positive or under investigation. During these events, chest compressions, manual ventilation (bag-valve-mask), and emergent intubation were performed, all of which carry aerosolization risks. Our Code Blue team includes ICU nurses, respiratory therapists, hospitalists, and the on-call anesthesiologist.

- Endotracheal intubation and extubation: These procedures are performed in the Emergency Department, ICU, and occasionally in the operating room (OR) for COVID-positive patients. Emergency physicians and anesthesiologists typically perform the intubations, with respiratory therapists assisting. Extubations typically occur in the ICU once patients have improved. We treat both intubation and extubation of suspected or confirmed COVID-19 cases as high-risk aerosol-generating procedures and follow enhanced PPE protocols.

- Non-invasive ventilation (e.g., BiPAP, CPAP): Respiratory therapists and ICU or ED nurses assist with initiation and ongoing management of non-invasive ventilation for some patients with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 who are in respiratory distress but not yet intubated. For example, we occasionally trial BiPAP in the ED or ICU when patients present with hypoxic respiratory failure and there is a potential benefit to avoiding intubation. These patients are placed in negative pressure rooms whenever possible, and staff use NIOSH-approved N95 respirators (or higher-level protection), eye protection, gowns, and gloves.

- Bronchoscopy: Our facility does perform bronchoscopies, but we have a standing policy to avoid routine bronchoscopy in suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients unless absolutely clinically necessary and after case-by-case review by pulmonology and infection prevention. At present, we have not performed bronchoscopies on COVID-positive patients in the last 12 months, so we are marking this as not routinely performed in that context.

- Manual ventilation: Manual ventilation with a bag-valve-mask device is performed during resuscitation events and occasionally during rapid sequence intubation for suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients. This is most common in the Emergency Department and ICU. Staff involved include respiratory therapists, physicians, and critical-care nurses.

- Medical, surgical, or postmortem procedures using oscillating bone saws: Our hospital does not maintain an on-site pathology lab that performs postmortem procedures with oscillating bone saws, and our OR rarely uses oscillating saws for cases involving known active COVID-19 infection. If such procedures are needed, they are generally scheduled when the patient is no longer infectious, or they are transferred to a regional center. For this reason, we have indicated that this is not currently performed on suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients.

- Dental AGPs: We do not have an on-site dental clinic and do not perform any dental procedures (including those involving ultrasonic scalers, high-speed dental handpieces, air/water syringes, air polishing, or air abrasion). Any dental needs are referred to off-site dental practices.

Summary of Yes/No Rationale:
- Direct care/exposure to suspected or confirmed COVID-19: true, because bedside nurses, respiratory therapists, physicians, and patient care technicians routinely care for patients under investigation or confirmed positive.
- Performance or assistance with AGPs: true, because our staff regularly perform open suctioning, CPR, intubation/extubation, non-invasive ventilation, and manual ventilation on suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients.

Additional Notes:
We maintain a designated cluster of negative pressure rooms in the ED and ICU for high-risk respiratory patients, including those with suspected or confirmed COVID-19. Employees performing AGPs are fit-tested for N95 respirators annually and trained in donning and doffing procedures. We also track which staff members participate in Code Blue events involving COVID-positive patients for post-exposure follow-up as needed.

answers.value:
This area is the primary intake location for patients presenting with acute respiratory symptoms, including those with suspected or confirmed COVID-19. Job roles and tasks include:

- Triage Nurses: Conduct initial assessments at close range, including measuring vital signs, performing pulse oximetry, asking detailed symptom and exposure history, and assisting patients with masks. They frequently come within less than 6 feet of coughing or short-of-breath patients.

- Emergency Department RNs: Provide ongoing bedside care such as administering IV medications (antibiotics, antivirals, steroids), drawing blood for labs, collecting nasopharyngeal swabs for COVID-19 testing, assisting with toileting and hygiene needs, and monitoring patients on continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitors. They frequently reposition patients, adjust oxygen delivery devices, and assist with proning.

- Emergency Physicians (Attending and Residents): Perform physical examinations, order and interpret diagnostic tests, and make decisions about respiratory support, including initiating high-flow oxygen or non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) for suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients. They also lead rapid sequence intubation for deteriorating patients.

- Respiratory Therapists: Set up and manage oxygen delivery systems (nasal cannula, non-rebreather masks), initiate and adjust non-invasive ventilation for respiratory distress, perform open suctioning of airways when necessary, assist with manual ventilation using bag-valve-mask devices, and prepare ventilators for intubated COVID-19 patients. They are often in very close proximity to patients' faces and airways.

- ED Technicians/Patient Care Assistants: Assist with transporting patients from triage to rooms, help patients change into hospital gowns, obtain EKGs, and perform basic hygiene care. They may also help position patients for imaging (e.g., portable chest X-rays) and assist nursing staff during emergent situations (e.g., holding patients during line placement).

These workers are present during high-risk aerosol-generating procedures, including CPR, manual ventilation, and endotracheal intubation. The ED respiratory pod is equipped with negative pressure rooms when available, but due to surge periods, standard rooms may occasionally be used, increasing the potential exposure risk for staff.

answers.value: Intensive Care Unit (ICU) – COVID-Capable Rooms (ICU-5 to ICU-10)
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The ICU COVID-capable rooms are where the most critically ill suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients are treated. Staff here have frequent and prolonged close contact and perform multiple aerosol-generating procedures.

- ICU Registered Nurses: Provide continuous bedside care, including titrating vasopressor drips, managing sedation and analgesia, monitoring invasive lines (central venous catheters, arterial lines), and performing comprehensive patient assessments. They perform or assist with open suctioning of airways (when closed systems are not feasible), assist in proning and supining intubated COVID-19 patients, and participate in emergent and planned extubations.

- Respiratory Therapists: Manage ventilator settings for intubated COVID-19 patients, perform ventilator checks, and adjust parameters based on arterial blood gas results and patient status. They perform manual ventilation with bag-valve-mask devices when transitioning patients off the ventilator (e.g., during transport or equipment changes) and assist anesthesiologists or intensivists during intubation and extubation. They also initiate and manage non-invasive ventilation in COVID-19 patients who are not intubated but require advanced respiratory support.

- Intensivists / Critical Care Physicians: Evaluate and manage overall care plans, including decisions to intubate or extubate COVID-19 patients. They perform endotracheal intubations, oversee extubations, and may participate directly in CPR when cardiac arrest occurs. They also perform bedside procedures such as central line insertion and arterial line placement, often in close proximity to patients' faces and upper airways.

- ICU Patient Care Technicians: Assist with turning, repositioning, and proning patients (which often requires several staff members working at close range). They help with bathing, changing linens, and providing basic hygiene, including oral care for intubated patients. They also assist nurses during emergent events, including Code Blue responses, by bringing equipment and sometimes helping with chest compressions.

Staff in this unit have some of the highest cumulative exposure times due to 12-hour shifts and repeated high-risk interventions. All COVID-capable ICU rooms are negative pressure when operationally available, but during high census, a mix of negative and standard pressure rooms may be in use. PPE includes N95 respirators or higher, eye protection (face shields or goggles), gowns, and gloves; powered air-purifying respirators (PAPRs) are available for staff who fail N95 fit testing.

Common AGPs performed in this location include open suctioning of airways, manual ventilation, endotracheal intubation, extubation, and participation in full resuscitation efforts (CPR) for decompensating COVID-19 patients.

answers.value:
The 4B respiratory cohort wing is a medical-surgical unit where patients with moderate respiratory symptoms, including those with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 who do not currently require ICU-level care, are treated.

- Med-Surg Nurses: Administer oral and IV medications, monitor vital signs and oxygen saturation, adjust low- to moderate-flow oxygen delivery devices (nasal cannula, simple masks), collect specimens for COVID-19 testing, and provide patient education about isolation precautions. They frequently assist patients in ambulating to the bathroom while on oxygen, help with meals, and support activities of daily living, all of which involve repeated close contact.

- Respiratory Therapists: Provide scheduled breathing treatments (using metered-dose inhalers with spacers instead of nebulizers for COVID-19 patients whenever possible), adjust oxygen flow rates, and evaluate patients for potential escalation of care (e.g., transfer to ICU if respiratory status worsens). They may occasionally assist with short periods of manual ventilation during transport or in emergencies on the unit.

- Hospitalists: Conduct daily rounds, perform physical examinations, and discuss treatment plans with patients and families (often via phone or video to reduce in-room time). They review labs and imaging and make decisions about escalation or de-escalation of respiratory support.

- Patient Care Technicians: Obtain vital signs, assist with bathing, toileting, and repositioning, and help with meal set-up. They are often in very close proximity to patients for extended periods, especially when patients are weak or confused and need hands-on assistance.

Although full-scale aerosol-generating procedures (such as intubation) are less common on this unit, staff are still at risk due to frequent exposure to coughing and breathing in shared airspace with COVID-19 patients, particularly during surges when rooms and hallways are busier. Occasional rapid response events take place here, during which CPR and manual ventilation may be initiated before transfer to the ICU.

answers.value:
Across the three main locations listed (Emergency Department Respiratory Pod, ICU COVID-capable rooms, and the Medical-Surgical Respiratory Cohort Wing), approximately 70 individual workers are routinely scheduled in roles that may involve exposure to suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients during a typical week. This includes full-time, part-time, and per diem staff.

Representative job tasks involving potential exposure include, but are not limited to:

1. Direct Bedside Care:
   - Taking vital signs and performing head-to-toe assessments at the bedside.
   - Assisting patients with activities of daily living (bathing, toileting, ambulation, feeding), often requiring physical support and close face-to-face proximity.
   - Performing oral care for intubated patients, which may provoke coughing.

2. Respiratory Support and Airway Management:
   - Initiating and adjusting supplemental oxygen delivery devices (nasal cannula, simple face masks, non-rebreather masks).
   - Setting up and managing non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) for patients with respiratory distress, including mask fitting and troubleshooting leaks, which can increase aerosol dispersion.
   - Performing or assisting with open suctioning of airways in situations where closed suction systems are not utilized or not effective.
   - Performing manual ventilation using bag-valve-mask devices during rapid deterioration, transport, or prior to/during intubation.
   - Assisting with or performing endotracheal intubation and extubation for COVID-19 patients in respiratory failure.

3. Emergency and Resuscitation Activities:
   - Participating in Code Blue and Rapid Response events for suspected or confirmed COVID-19 patients, including chest compressions during CPR, manual ventilation, and rapid sequence intubation.
   - Transporting unstable COVID-19 patients between units (e.g., ED to ICU, ICU to imaging), during which airway devices may need to be manually supported.

4. Diagnostic and Specimen Collection Tasks:
   - Obtaining nasopharyngeal and oropharyngeal swabs for COVID-19 testing, which often triggers coughing or gagging.
   - Drawing blood and placing IV lines while in close proximity to symptomatic patients.
   - Assisting with portable imaging (e.g., chest X-rays) that requires physically positioning the patient.

5. Environmental and Support Activities:
   - Cleaning and disinfecting patient rooms and high-touch surfaces following discharge of COVID-19 patients (primarily environmental services staff, but nursing staff often assist with room turnover in high-volume periods).
   - Handling soiled linens, meal trays, and medical waste from isolation rooms.

In all of these tasks, employees are instructed to use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) based on our hospital’s COVID-19 protocol, which includes N95 respirators (or higher-level protection) for any aerosol-generating procedure or care in high-risk areas, as well as eye protection, gowns, and gloves. Staff receive initial and annual refresher training on infection prevention, donning and doffing PPE, and recognition and reporting of potential occupational exposures.
